From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

Genre of popular music

This article is about the 1950s style of music. For the general rock music genre, see [Rock music](/wiki/Rock_music "Rock music"). For other uses, see [Rock and roll (disambiguation)](/wiki/Rock_and_roll_(disambiguation) "Rock and roll (disambiguation)"). "RnR" redirects here. For other uses, see [RNR (disambiguation)](/wiki/RNR_(disambiguation) "RNR (disambiguation)").

**Rock and roll**[[a]](#cite_note-1) is a [genre](/wiki/Genre_(music) "Genre (music)") of [popular music](/wiki/Popular_music "Popular music") that evolved in the [United States](/wiki/United_States "United States") during the late 1940s and early 1950s.[[1]](#cite_note-:0-2)[[2]](#cite_note-dawson_propes-3) The [origins of rock and roll](/wiki/Origins_of_rock_and_roll "Origins of rock and roll") are rooted in a blending of [African-American musical](/wiki/African-American_music "African-American music") genres, mainly [rhythm and blues](/wiki/Rhythm_and_blues "Rhythm and blues"), with stylistics influences from [gospel](/wiki/Gospel_music "Gospel music"), [jazz](/wiki/Jazz "Jazz"), [boogie-woogie](/wiki/Boogie-woogie "Boogie-woogie"), [electric blues](/wiki/Electric_blues "Electric blues"), [jump blues](/wiki/Jump_blues "Jump blues"), [swing](/wiki/Swing_music "Swing music"), and [folk music](/wiki/Folk_music "Folk music").[[3]](#cite_note-4)[[4]](#cite_note-5) While rock and roll's early elements can be heard in [blues](/wiki/Blues "Blues") records from the 1920s[[5]](#cite_note-6) and in [country](/wiki/Country_music "Country music") records of the 1930s,[[6]](#cite_note-Peterson-7) the genre did not acquire its name until 1954.[[7]](#cite_note-8)[[2]](#cite_note-dawson_propes-3)

By the mid-1960s, rock and roll had developed into "the more encompassing international style known as [rock music](/wiki/Rock_music "Rock music"), though the latter also continued to be known in many circles as rock and roll".[[8]](#cite_note-kot-eb-9) For the purpose of differentiation, this article deals with the first definition.

In the earliest *rock and roll* styles, either the [piano](/wiki/Piano "Piano") or [saxophone](/wiki/Saxophone "Saxophone") was typically the lead instrument. These instruments were generally replaced or supplemented by the [electric guitar](/wiki/Electric_guitar "Electric guitar") in the mid-to-late 1950s.[[9]](#cite_note-Evans2002-10) The beat is essentially a dance rhythm[[10]](#cite_note-11) with an accentuated [backbeat](/wiki/Backbeat "Backbeat"), almost always provided by a [snare drum](/wiki/Snare_drum "Snare drum").[[11]](#cite_note-12) Minimal blues [chord progressions](/wiki/Chord_progression "Chord progression") such as the [twelve-bar blues](/wiki/Twelve-bar_blues "Twelve-bar blues") are commonly used.[[12]](#cite_note-13) Classic rock and roll is usually played with one or more [electric guitars](/wiki/Electric_guitar "Electric guitar") (one [lead](/wiki/Lead_guitar "Lead guitar"), one [rhythm](/wiki/Rhythm_guitar "Rhythm guitar")) and a [double bass](/wiki/Double_bass "Double bass") (string bass). After the mid-1950s, electric [bass guitars](/wiki/Bass_guitar "Bass guitar") (first mass-produced by [Fender](/wiki/Fender_(company) "Fender (company)") in the early 1950s) and [drum kits](/wiki/Drum_kit "Drum kit") became popular in classic rock.[[9]](#cite_note-Evans2002-10)

Rock and roll had a profound influence on contemporary American lifestyles, fashion, attitudes, and language, and is often portrayed in movies, fan magazines, and on television. According to G. C Altschuler the music had a positive influence on the [civil rights movement](/wiki/Civil_rights_movement "Civil rights movement"), because of its widespread appeal to both [Black American](/wiki/Black_American "Black American") and [White American](/wiki/White_American "White American") teenagers.[[13]](#cite_note-Altshuler2003p35-14)[[14]](#cite_note-15)

## Terminology

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Rock_and_roll&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Terminology")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/f0/Birthplace_of_Rock_%27N%27_Roll.jpg/250px-Birthplace_of_Rock_%27N%27_Roll.jpg)](/wiki/File:Birthplace_of_Rock_%27N%27_Roll.jpg)

Sign commemorating the role of [Alan Freed](/wiki/Alan_Freed "Alan Freed") and [Cleveland](/wiki/Cleveland "Cleveland"), Ohio, in the origins of rock and roll

The term "rock and roll" is defined by [Greg Kot](/wiki/Greg_Kot "Greg Kot") in *[Encyclopædia Britannica](/wiki/Encyclop%C3%A6dia_Britannica "Encyclopædia Britannica")* as the music that originated in the mid-1950s and later developed "into the more encompassing international style known as [rock music](/wiki/Rock_music "Rock music")".[[8]](#cite_note-kot-eb-9) The term is sometimes also used as [synonymous](/wiki/Synonymous "Synonymous") with "rock music" and is defined as such in some dictionaries.[[15]](#cite_note-16)[[16]](#cite_note-17)

The phrase "rocking and rolling" originally described the movement of a ship on the ocean,[[17]](#cite_note-18) but by the early 20th century was used both to describe the spiritual fervor of black church rituals[[18]](#cite_note-hoy-19) and as a sexual analogy. A retired [Welsh](/wiki/Wales "Wales") seaman named William Fender can be heard singing the phrase "rock and roll" when describing a sexual encounter in his performance of the traditional song "[The Baffled Knight](/wiki/The_Baffled_Knight "The Baffled Knight")" to the folklorist [James Madison Carpenter](/wiki/James_Madison_Carpenter "James Madison Carpenter") in the early 1930s, which he would have learned at sea in the 1800s; the recording can be heard on the [Vaughan Williams Memorial Library](/wiki/Vaughan_Williams_Memorial_Library "Vaughan Williams Memorial Library") website.[[19]](#cite_note-20)

Various gospel, blues and swing recordings used the phrase before it became widely popular. "[Bosom of Abraham](/wiki/Bosom_of_Abraham "Bosom of Abraham")", an African-American [spiritual](/wiki/Spirituals "Spirituals") that was documented no later than 1867 (just after the [Civil War](/wiki/American_Civil_War "American Civil War")), uses the phrase "rock my soul" frequently in a religious sense; this song was later recorded by musicians from various genres, including various gospel musicians and groups (including [The Jordanaires](/wiki/The_Jordanaires "The Jordanaires")), [Louis Armstrong](/wiki/Louis_Armstrong "Louis Armstrong") (jazz/swing), [Lonnie Donegan](/wiki/Lonnie_Donegan "Lonnie Donegan") ([skiffle](/wiki/Skiffle "Skiffle")), and [Elvis Presley](/wiki/Elvis_Presley "Elvis Presley") (rock and roll/pop/country).[[20]](#cite_note-AWG_1867-21) Blues singer [Trixie Smith](/wiki/Trixie_Smith "Trixie Smith") recorded "My Man Rocks Me with One Steady Roll"[[21]](#cite_note-22) in 1922. It was used in 1940s recordings and reviews of what became known as "[rhythm and blues](/wiki/Rhythm_and_blues "Rhythm and blues")" music aimed at a black audience.[[18]](#cite_note-hoy-19) [Huey "Piano" Smith](/wiki/Huey_%22Piano%22_Smith "Huey \"Piano\" Smith") credits [Cha Cha Hogan](/wiki/Cha_Cha_Hogan "Cha Cha Hogan"), a jump-blues shouter and comic in New Orleans, with popularizing the term in his 1950 song "My Walking Baby".[[22]](#cite_note-Wirt2014-23)[[23]](#cite_note-JazzArchivist2015-24)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/ef/Alan_Freed_1957.JPG/250px-Alan_Freed_1957.JPG)](/wiki/File:Alan_Freed_1957.JPG)

Disc jockey [Alan Freed](/wiki/Alan_Freed "Alan Freed") is credited with popularizing the term "rock and roll" and introducing the genre across racial barriers in the 1950s

In 1934, the song "Rock and Roll" by the [Boswell Sisters](/wiki/Boswell_Sisters "Boswell Sisters") appeared in the film *[Transatlantic Merry-Go-Round](/wiki/Transatlantic_Merry-Go-Round "Transatlantic Merry-Go-Round")*. In 1942, before the concept of rock and roll had been defined, *[Billboard](/wiki/Billboard_(magazine) "Billboard (magazine)")* magazine columnist [Maurie Orodenker](/wiki/Maurie_Orodenker "Maurie Orodenker") started to use the term to describe upbeat recordings such as "Rock Me" by [Sister Rosetta Tharpe](/wiki/Sister_Rosetta_Tharpe "Sister Rosetta Tharpe"); her style on that recording was described as "rock-and-roll spiritual singing".[[24]](#cite_note-25)[[25]](#cite_note-26) By 1943, the "Rock and Roll Inn" in [South Merchantville, New Jersey](/wiki/Merchantville,_New_Jersey "Merchantville, New Jersey"), was established as a music venue.[[26]](#cite_note-27) In 1951, [Cleveland](/wiki/Cleveland "Cleveland"), Ohio, disc jockey [Alan Freed](/wiki/Alan_Freed "Alan Freed") began playing this music style, and referring to it as "rock and roll"[[27]](#cite_note-28) on his mainstream radio program, which popularized the phrase.[[28]](#cite_note-29)

Several sources suggest that Freed found the term, used as a synonym for sexual intercourse, on the record "[Sixty Minute Man](/wiki/Sixty_Minute_Man "Sixty Minute Man")" by [Billy Ward and his Dominoes](/wiki/Billy_Ward_and_his_Dominoes "Billy Ward and his Dominoes").[[29]](#cite_note-30)[[30]](#cite_note-31) The lyrics include the line, "I rock 'em, roll 'em all night long".[[31]](#cite_note-32) Freed did not acknowledge the suggestion about that source in interviews, and explained the term as follows: "Rock 'n roll is really swing with a modern name. It began on the levees and plantations, took in folk songs, and features blues and rhythm".[[32]](#cite_note-33)

In discussing Alan Freed's contribution to the genre, two significant sources emphasized the importance of African-American rhythm and blues. Greg Harris, then the executive director of the Rock n Roll Hall of Fame, offered this comment to [CNN](/wiki/CNN "CNN"): "Freed's role in breaking down racial barriers in American pop culture in the 1950s, by leading white and black kids to listen to the same music, put the radio personality 'at the vanguard' and made him 'a really important figure'".[[33]](#cite_note-34) After Freed was honored with a star on the [Hollywood Walk of Fame](/wiki/Hollywood_Walk_of_Fame "Hollywood Walk of Fame"), the organization's Web site offered this comment: "He became internationally known for promoting African-American rhythm and blues music on the radio in the United States and Europe under the name of rock and roll".[[34]](#cite_note-35)

Not often acknowledged in the history of rock and roll, [Todd Storz](/wiki/Todd_Storz "Todd Storz"), the owner of radio station KOWH in [Omaha](/wiki/Omaha "Omaha"), Nebraska, was the first to adopt the [Top 40](/wiki/Top_40 "Top 40") format (in 1953), playing only the most popular records in rotation. His station, and the numerous others which adopted the concept, helped to promote the genre: by the mid 50s, the playlist included artists such as "[Presley](/wiki/Elvis_Presley "Elvis Presley"), [Lewis](/wiki/Jerry_Lee_Lewis "Jerry Lee Lewis"), [Haley](/wiki/Bill_Haley "Bill Haley"), [Berry](/wiki/Chuck_Berry "Chuck Berry") and [Domino](/wiki/Fats_Domino "Fats Domino")".[[35]](#cite_note-36)[[36]](#cite_note-37)

## Early rock and roll

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Rock_and_roll&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Early rock and roll")]

### Origins

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Rock_and_roll&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Origins")]

Main article: [Origins of rock and roll](/wiki/Origins_of_rock_and_roll "Origins of rock and roll")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/20/Chuck_Berry_1957.jpg/250px-Chuck_Berry_1957.jpg)](/wiki/File:Chuck_Berry_1957.jpg)

[Chuck Berry](/wiki/Chuck_Berry "Chuck Berry") in 1957

While the history has been subject to debate, modern scholarship increasingly centers the genre as a direct descendant of the black experience in the American South and the urban centers of the North.[[37]](#cite_note-AllmusicR&R-38) At its core, rock and roll is the electrification and acceleration of African-American musical traditions. While European instrumentation provided the physical tools; the rhythm, structure, and soul of the genre were forged in the black community.[[38]](#cite_note-39)[[39]](#cite_note-40) [The migration of many former slaves and their descendants to major urban centers](/wiki/Second_Great_Migration_(African_American) "Second Great Migration (African American)") such as [St. Louis](/wiki/St._Louis "St. Louis"), [Memphis](/wiki/Memphis,_Tennessee "Memphis, Tennessee"), [New York City](/wiki/New_York_City "New York City"), [Detroit](/wiki/Detroit "Detroit"), [Chicago](/wiki/Chicago "Chicago"), [Cleveland](/wiki/Cleveland "Cleveland"), and [Buffalo](/wiki/Buffalo,_New_York "Buffalo, New York") meant that black and white residents were living in close proximity in larger numbers than ever before.[[40]](#cite_note-41)[[41]](#cite_note-42) Radio stations that made white and black forms of music available to both groups, the development and spread of the [gramophone record](/wiki/Gramophone_record "Gramophone record"), and African-American musical styles such as [jazz](/wiki/Jazz "Jazz") and [swing](/wiki/Swing_music "Swing music") which were taken up by white musicians, aided this process of "cultural collision".[[42]](#cite_note-Bertrand2000-43)

The immediate roots of rock and roll lay in the [rhythm and blues](/wiki/Rhythm_and_blues "Rhythm and blues"), then called "[race music](/wiki/Race_music "Race music")",[[43]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGilliland1969show_3,_show_55-44) in combination with either boogie-woogie and shouting gospel[[44]](#cite_note-reuters.com-45) or with [country music](/wiki/Country_music "Country music") of the 1940s and 1950s. Particularly significant influences were jazz, [blues](/wiki/Blues "Blues"), [gospel](/wiki/Gospel_music "Gospel music"), country, and [folk](/wiki/Folk_music "Folk music").[[37]](#cite_note-AllmusicR&R-38) Commentators differ in their views of which of these forms were most important and the degree to which the new music was a re-branding of African-American [rhythm and blues](/wiki/Rhythm_and_blues "Rhythm and blues") for a white market, or a new hybrid of black and white forms.[[45]](#cite_note-46)[[46]](#cite_note-KeightleyR&R-47)[[47]](#cite_note-48)

[![A picture of the 7" single for "Roll 'Em Pete"](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/4d/Roll_Em_Pete.jpg/250px-Roll_Em_Pete.jpg)](/wiki/File:Roll_Em_Pete.jpg)

[Big Joe Turner](/wiki/Big_Joe_Turner "Big Joe Turner") and [Pete Johnson](/wiki/Pete_Johnson_(musician) "Pete Johnson (musician)")'s record "[Roll 'Em Pete](/wiki/Roll_%27Em_Pete "Roll 'Em Pete")" is regarded as a precursor to rock and roll.

In the 1930s, [jazz](/wiki/Jazz "Jazz"), and particularly [swing](/wiki/Swing_music "Swing music"), both in urban-based dance bands and blues-influenced country swing were among the first music to present African-American sounds for a predominantly white audience.[[46]](#cite_note-KeightleyR&R-47)[[48]](#cite_note-49) One particularly noteworthy example of a jazz song with recognizably rock and roll elements is [Big Joe Turner](/wiki/Big_Joe_Turner "Big Joe Turner") with pianist [Pete Johnson](/wiki/Pete_Johnson_(musician) "Pete Johnson (musician)")'s 1938 single "[Roll 'Em Pete](/wiki/Roll_%27Em_Pete "Roll 'Em Pete")", which is regarded as an important precursor of rock and roll.[[49]](#cite_note-50)[[50]](#cite_note-51)[[51]](#cite_note-52) The 1940s saw the increased use of blaring horns (including saxophones), shouted lyrics and boogie-woogie beats in jazz-based music. During and immediately after [World War II](/wiki/World_War_II "World War II"), with shortages of fuel and limitations on audiences and available personnel, large jazz bands were less economical and tended to be replaced by smaller combos, using guitars, bass and drums.[[37]](#cite_note-AllmusicR&R-38)[[52]](#cite_note-53) In the same period, particularly on the [West Coast](/wiki/West_Coast_of_the_United_States "West Coast of the United States") and in the [Midwest](/wiki/Midwestern_United_States "Midwestern United States"), the development of [jump blues](/wiki/Jump_blues "Jump blues"), with its guitar riffs, prominent beats and shouted lyrics, prefigured many later developments.[[37]](#cite_note-AllmusicR&R-38) In the documentary film *[Hail! Hail! Rock 'n' Roll](/wiki/Hail!_Hail!_Rock_%27n%27_Roll "Hail! Hail! Rock 'n' Roll")*, [Keith Richards](/wiki/Keith_Richards "Keith Richards") proposes that [Chuck Berry](/wiki/Chuck_Berry "Chuck Berry") developed his brand of rock and roll by transposing the familiar two-note lead line of jump blues piano directly to the electric guitar, creating what is instantly recognizable as rock guitar. This proposal by Richards neglects the black guitarists who did the same thing before Berry, such as [Goree Carter](/wiki/Goree_Carter "Goree Carter"),[[53]](#cite_note-54) [Gatemouth Brown](/wiki/Clarence_%22Gatemouth%22_Brown "Clarence \"Gatemouth\" Brown"),[[54]](#cite_note-55) and the originator of the style, [T-Bone Walker](/wiki/T-Bone_Walker "T-Bone Walker").[[55]](#cite_note-56) [Country boogie](/wiki/Country_boogie "Country boogie") and [Chicago electric blues](/wiki/Chicago_blues "Chicago blues") supplied many of the elements that would be seen as characteristic of rock and roll.[[37]](#cite_note-AllmusicR&R-38) Inspired by [electric blues](/wiki/Electric_blues "Electric blues"), Chuck Berry introduced an aggressive guitar sound to rock and roll, and established the electric guitar as its centerpiece,[[56]](#cite_note-57) adapting his rock band instrumentation from the basic blues band instrumentation of a lead guitar, second chord instrument, bass and drums.[[57]](#cite_note-campbell-58) In 2017, [Robert Christgau](/wiki/Robert_Christgau "Robert Christgau") declared that "Chuck Berry did in fact invent rock 'n' roll", explaining that this artist "came the closest of any single figure to being the one who put all the essential pieces together".[[58]](#cite_note-59)

Rock and roll arrived at a time of considerable technological change, soon after the development of the electric guitar, [amplifier](/wiki/Amplifier "Amplifier"), [45 rpm record](/wiki/45_rpm_record "45 rpm record") and modern condenser [microphones](/wiki/Microphone "Microphone").[[37]](#cite_note-AllmusicR&R-38) There were also changes in the record industry, with the rise of independent labels like [Atlantic](/wiki/Atlantic_records "Atlantic records"), [Sun](/wiki/Sun_Records "Sun Records") and [Chess](/wiki/Chess_Records "Chess Records") servicing [niche audiences](/wiki/Niche_market "Niche market") and a similar rise of radio stations that played their music.[[37]](#cite_note-AllmusicR&R-38) It was the realization that relatively affluent white teenagers were listening to this music that led to the development of what was to be defined as rock and roll as a distinct genre.[[37]](#cite_note-AllmusicR&R-38) Because the development of rock and roll was an evolutionary process, no single record can be identified as unambiguously "the first" rock and roll record.[[2]](#cite_note-dawson_propes-3) Contenders for the title of "[first rock and roll record](/wiki/First_rock_and_roll_record "First rock and roll record")" include [Sister Rosetta Tharpe](/wiki/Sister_Rosetta_Tharpe "Sister Rosetta Tharpe")'s "[Strange Things Happening Every Day](/wiki/Strange_Things_Happening_Every_Day "Strange Things Happening Every Day")" (1944),[[59]](#cite_note-60) "[That's All Right](/wiki/That%27s_All_Right "That's All Right")" by [Arthur Crudup](/wiki/Arthur_Crudup "Arthur Crudup") (1946), "[Move It On Over](/wiki/Move_It_On_Over_(song) "Move It On Over (song)")" by [Hank Williams](/wiki/Hank_Williams "Hank Williams") (1947),[[60]](#cite_note-61) "[The Fat Man](/wiki/The_Fat_Man_(song) "The Fat Man (song)")" by [Fats Domino](/wiki/Fats_Domino "Fats Domino") (1949),[[2]](#cite_note-dawson_propes-3) [Goree Carter](/wiki/Goree_Carter "Goree Carter")'s "[Rock Awhile](/wiki/Rock_Awhile "Rock Awhile")" (1949),[[61]](#cite_note-palmer1992p19-62) and [Jimmy Preston](/wiki/Jimmy_Preston "Jimmy Preston")'s "[Rock the Joint](/wiki/Rock_the_Joint "Rock the Joint")" (1949) (later [covered](/wiki/Cover_version "Cover version") by [Bill Haley & His Comets](/wiki/Bill_Haley_%26_His_Comets "Bill Haley & His Comets") in 1952).[[62]](#cite_note-63)

"[Rocket 88](/wiki/Rocket_88 "Rocket 88")" by [Jackie Brenston](/wiki/Jackie_Brenston "Jackie Brenston") and his Delta Cats ([Ike Turner](/wiki/Ike_Turner "Ike Turner") and his band [The Kings of Rhythm](/wiki/Kings_of_Rhythm "Kings of Rhythm") and sung by Brenston), was recorded by [Sam Phillips](/wiki/Sam_Phillips "Sam Phillips") in March 1951. This is often cited as the first rock n' roll record.[[63]](#cite_note-theguardian_com-64)[[64]](#cite_note-Campbell2008pp157-8-65) In an interview however, Ike Turner offered this comment: "I don't think that 'Rocket 88' is rock 'n' roll. I think that 'Rocket 88' is R&B, but I think 'Rocket 88' is the cause of rock and roll existing".[[65]](#cite_note-66)[[66]](#cite_note-67)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/0e/BillHaley.JPG/330px-BillHaley.JPG)](/wiki/File:BillHaley.JPG)

[Bill Haley](/wiki/Bill_Haley "Bill Haley") and his Comets performing in the 1954 Universal International film *Round Up of Rhythm*

In terms of its wide cultural impact across society in the US and elsewhere, [Bill Haley](/wiki/Bill_Haley_(musician) "Bill Haley (musician)")'s "[Rock Around the Clock](/wiki/Rock_Around_the_Clock "Rock Around the Clock")",[[67]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGilliland1969show_5,_show_55-68) recorded in April 1954 but not a commercial success until the following year, is generally recognized as an important milestone, but it was preceded by many recordings from earlier decades in which elements of rock and roll can be clearly discerned.[[2]](#cite_note-dawson_propes-3)[[68]](#cite_note-palmer1980pp3-14-69)[[69]](#cite_note-unterberger_birth-70)

Journalist [Alexis Petridis](/wiki/Alexis_Petridis "Alexis Petridis") argued that neither Haley's "Rock Around the Clock" nor Presley's version of "That's Alright Mama" heralded a new genre: "They were simply the first white artists' interpretations of a sound already well-established by black musicians almost a decade before. It was a raucous, driving, unnamed variant of rhythm and blues that came complete with lyrics that talked about rocking".[[63]](#cite_note-theguardian_com-64)

Other artists with early rock and roll hits included [Chuck Berry](/wiki/Chuck_Berry "Chuck Berry"), [Bo Diddley](/wiki/Bo_Diddley "Bo Diddley"), [Little Richard](/wiki/Little_Richard "Little Richard"), [Jerry Lee Lewis](/wiki/Jerry_Lee_Lewis "Jerry Lee Lewis"), and [Gene Vincent](/wiki/Gene_Vincent "Gene Vincent").[[64]](#cite_note-Campbell2008pp157-8-65) Chuck Berry's 1955 classic "[Maybellene](/wiki/Maybellene "Maybellene")" in particular features a [distorted](/wiki/Distortion_(music) "Distortion (music)") [electric guitar](/wiki/Electric_guitar "Electric guitar") solo with warm [overtones](/wiki/Overtone "Overtone") created by his small [valve amplifier](/wiki/Valve_amplifier "Valve amplifier").[[70]](#cite_note-71) However, the use of distortion was predated by electric blues guitarists such as [Joe Hill Louis](/wiki/Joe_Hill_Louis "Joe Hill Louis"),[[71]](#cite_note-72) [Guitar Slim](/wiki/Guitar_Slim "Guitar Slim"),[[72]](#cite_note-Aswell2010-73) [Willie Johnson](/wiki/Willie_Johnson_(guitarist) "Willie Johnson (guitarist)") of [Howlin' Wolf](/wiki/Howlin%27_Wolf "Howlin' Wolf")'s band,[[73]](#cite_note-Rubin-74) and [Pat Hare](/wiki/Pat_Hare "Pat Hare"); the latter two also made use of distorted [power chords](/wiki/Power_chord "Power chord") in the early 1950s.[[74]](#cite_note-palmer1992p24-27-75) Also in 1955, Bo Diddley introduced the "[Bo Diddley beat](/wiki/Bo_Diddley_beat "Bo Diddley beat")" and a unique electric guitar style,[[75]](#cite_note-76) influenced by [African](/wiki/Music_of_Africa "Music of Africa") and [Afro-Cuban music](/wiki/Afro-Cuban_music "Afro-Cuban music") and in turn influencing many later artists.[[76]](#cite_note-77)[[77]](#cite_note-78)[[78]](#cite_note-independent_bo-79)

### Rhythm and blues

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Rock_and_roll&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Rhythm and blues")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/fb/LaVern_Baker%2C_1957_closeup.jpg/250px-LaVern_Baker%2C_1957_closeup.jpg)](/wiki/File:LaVern_Baker,_1957_closeup.jpg)

[LaVern Baker](/wiki/LaVern_Baker "LaVern Baker") was an R&B singer who was influential on rock and roll

Rock and roll was strongly influenced by R&B, according to many sources, including an article in *[The Wall Street Journal](/wiki/The_Wall_Street_Journal "The Wall Street Journal")* in 1985, titled, "Rock! It's Still Rhythm and Blues". In fact, the author stated that the "two terms were used interchangeably", until about 1957. The other sources quoted in the article said that rock and roll combined R&B with pop and country music.[[79]](#cite_note-80)

[Fats Domino](/wiki/Fats_Domino "Fats Domino") was one of the biggest stars of rock and roll in the early 1950s and he was not convinced that this was a new genre. In 1957, he said: "What they call rock 'n' roll now is rhythm and blues. I've been playing it for 15 years in New Orleans".[[80]](#cite_note-81) According to *[Rolling Stone](/wiki/Rolling_Stone_(magazine) "Rolling Stone (magazine)")*, "this is a valid statement ... all Fifties rockers, black and white, country born and city-bred, were fundamentally influenced by R&B, the black popular music of the late Forties and early Fifties".[[81]](#cite_note-82) Further, [Little Richard](/wiki/Little_Richard "Little Richard") built his ground-breaking sound of the same era with an uptempo blend of boogie-woogie, New Orleans rhythm and blues, and the soul and fervor of gospel music vocalization.[[44]](#cite_note-reuters.com-45)

Less frequently cited as an influencer, [LaVern Baker](/wiki/LaVern_Baker "LaVern Baker") was inducted into the Rock and Roll Hall of Fame in 1991. The Hall remarked that her "fiery fusion of blues, jazz and R&B showcased her alluring vocals and set the stage for the rock and roll surge of the Fifties".[[82]](#cite_note-83)

### Rockabilly

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Rock_and_roll&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Rockabilly")]

Main article: [Rockabilly](/wiki/Rockabilly "Rockabilly")

[![A black and white photograph of Elvis Presley standing between two sets of bars](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/99/Elvis_Presley_promoting_Jailhouse_Rock.jpg/250px-Elvis_Presley_promoting_Jailhouse_Rock.jpg)](/wiki/File:Elvis_Presley_promoting_Jailhouse_Rock.jpg)

[Elvis Presley](/wiki/Elvis_Presley "Elvis Presley") in a promotion shot for *[Jailhouse Rock](/wiki/Jailhouse_Rock_(film) "Jailhouse Rock (film)")* in 1957

"Rockabilly" usually (but not exclusively) refers to the type of rock and roll music which was played and recorded in the mid-1950s primarily by white singers such as [Elvis Presley](/wiki/Elvis_Presley "Elvis Presley"), [Carl Perkins](/wiki/Carl_Perkins "Carl Perkins"), [Johnny Cash](/wiki/Johnny_Cash "Johnny Cash"), and [Jerry Lee Lewis](/wiki/Jerry_Lee_Lewis "Jerry Lee Lewis"), who drew mainly on the country roots of the music.[[83]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGilliland1969shows_7–8-84)[[84]](#cite_note-AllmusicRbilly-85) Presley was greatly influenced by and incorporated his style of music with that of some of the greatest Black musicians like BB King, Arthur Crudup and Fats Domino. His style of music combined with black influences created controversy during a turbulent time in history.[[84]](#cite_note-AllmusicRbilly-85) Many other popular rock and roll singers of the time, such as [Fats Domino](/wiki/Fats_Domino "Fats Domino") and [Little Richard](/wiki/Little_Richard "Little Richard"),[[85]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGilliland1969show_6-86) came out of the black [rhythm and blues](/wiki/Rhythm_and_blues "Rhythm and blues") tradition, making the music attractive to white audiences, and are not usually classed as "rockabilly".

Presley popularized rock and roll on a wider scale than any other single performer, and by 1956, he had emerged as the singing sensation of the nation.[[86]](#cite_note-87)

[Bill Flagg](/wiki/Bill_Flagg "Bill Flagg"), a resident of Connecticut, began referring to his mix of hillbilly and rock 'n' roll music as rockabilly around 1953.[[87]](#cite_note-88)

In July 1954, Presley recorded the regional hit "[That's All Right](/wiki/That%27s_All_Right "That's All Right")" at Sam Phillips' [Sun Studio](/wiki/Sun_Studio "Sun Studio") in Memphis.[[88]](#cite_note-AllmusicElvis-89) Three months earlier, on April 12, 1954, [Bill Haley & His Comets](/wiki/Bill_Haley_%26_His_Comets "Bill Haley & His Comets") recorded "Rock Around the Clock". Although only a minor hit when first released, when used in the opening sequence of the movie *[Blackboard Jungle](/wiki/Blackboard_Jungle "Blackboard Jungle")* a year later, it set the rock and roll boom in motion.[[67]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGilliland1969show_5,_show_55-68) The song became one of the biggest hits in history, and frenzied teens flocked to see Haley and the Comets perform it, causing riots in some cities. "Rock Around the Clock" was a breakthrough success for the group; traditionally, the song has been seen as the major breakthrough for the rock and roll genre, as its immense popularity introduced the music to a global audience.[[89]](#cite_note-AllmusicHaley-90) Haley and the Comets' earlier hit "[Crazy Man, Crazy](/wiki/Crazy_Man,_Crazy "Crazy Man, Crazy")" is recognized as the first rock and roll song to hit the mainstream charts, peaking at No. 15 on the *[Billboard](/wiki/Billboard_(magazine) "Billboard (magazine)")* singles chart in May 1953, and notably features guitar fills in place of piano or saxophone.[[90]](#cite_note-91)

In 1956, the arrival of rockabilly was underlined by the success of songs like "[Folsom Prison Blues](/wiki/Folsom_Prison_Blues "Folsom Prison Blues")" by [Johnny Cash](/wiki/Johnny_Cash "Johnny Cash"), "[Blue Suede Shoes](/wiki/Blue_Suede_Shoes "Blue Suede Shoes")" by Perkins, and the No. 1 hit "[Heartbreak Hotel](/wiki/Heartbreak_Hotel "Heartbreak Hotel")" by Presley.[[84]](#cite_note-AllmusicRbilly-85) For a few years it became the most commercially successful form of rock and roll. Later rockabilly acts, particularly performing songwriters like [Buddy Holly](/wiki/Buddy_Holly "Buddy Holly"), would be a major influence on [British Invasion](/wiki/British_Invasion "British Invasion") acts and particularly on the song writing of [the Beatles](/wiki/The_Beatles "The Beatles") and through them on the nature of later rock music.[[91]](#cite_note-92)

### Cover versions

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Rock_and_roll&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Cover versions")]

Main article: [Cover version](/wiki/Cover_version "Cover version")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d5/Little_Richard_1957_%28cropped%29.JPG/250px-Little_Richard_1957_%28cropped%29.JPG)](/wiki/File:Little_Richard_1957_(cropped).JPG)

[Little Richard](/wiki/Little_Richard "Little Richard") in 1957

Many of the earliest white rock and roll hits were [covers](/wiki/Cover_version "Cover version") or partial re-writes of earlier black rhythm and blues or blues songs.[[92]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGilliland1969show_4,_track_5-93) Through the late 1940s and early 1950s, [R&B](/wiki/Rhythm_and_blues "Rhythm and blues") music had been gaining a stronger beat and a wilder style, with artists such as Fats Domino and [Johnny Otis](/wiki/Johnny_Otis "Johnny Otis") speeding up the [tempos](/wiki/Tempos "Tempos") and increasing the [backbeat](/wiki/Beat_(music) "Beat (music)") to great popularity on the [juke joint](/wiki/Juke_joint "Juke joint") circuit.[[93]](#cite_note-94) Before the efforts of Freed and others, black music was taboo on many white-owned radio outlets, but artists and producers quickly recognized the potential of rock and roll.[[94]](#cite_note-95) Some of Presley's early recordings were covers of black rhythm and blues or blues songs, such as "[That's All Right](/wiki/That%27s_All_Right "That's All Right")" (a countrified arrangement of a blues number), "[Baby Let's Play House](/wiki/Baby_Let%27s_Play_House "Baby Let's Play House")", "[Lawdy Miss Clawdy](/wiki/Lawdy_Miss_Clawdy "Lawdy Miss Clawdy")", and "[Hound Dog](/wiki/Hound_Dog_(song) "Hound Dog (song)")".[[95]](#cite_note-96) The racial lines, however, are rather more clouded by the fact that some of these R&B songs originally recorded by black artists had been written by white songwriters, such as the team of [Jerry Leiber and Mike Stoller](/wiki/Jerry_Leiber_and_Mike_Stoller "Jerry Leiber and Mike Stoller"). Songwriting credits were often unreliable; many publishers, record executives, and even managers (both white and black) would insert their name as a composer in order to collect royalty checks.

Covers were customary in the music industry at the time; it was made particularly easy by the [compulsory license](/wiki/Compulsory_license "Compulsory license") provision of [United States copyright law](/wiki/United_States_copyright_law "United States copyright law") (still in effect).[[96]](#cite_note-97) One of the first relevant successful covers was [Wynonie Harris](/wiki/Wynonie_Harris "Wynonie Harris")'s transformation of [Roy Brown](/wiki/Roy_Brown_(blues_musician) "Roy Brown (blues musician)")'s 1947 original jump blues hit "[Good Rocking Tonight](/wiki/Good_Rocking_Tonight "Good Rocking Tonight")" into a more showy rocker[[97]](#cite_note-98) and the Louis Prima rocker "Oh Babe" in 1950, as well as [Amos Milburn](/wiki/Amos_Milburn "Amos Milburn")'s cover of what may have been the first white rock and roll record, [Hardrock Gunter](/wiki/Hardrock_Gunter "Hardrock Gunter")'s "Birmingham Bounce" in 1949.[[98]](#cite_note-99) The most notable trend, however, was white pop covers of black R&B numbers. The more familiar sound of these covers may have been more palatable to white audiences, there may have been an element of prejudice, but labels aimed at the white market also had much better distribution networks and were generally much more profitable.[[99]](#cite_note-100) Famously, [Pat Boone](/wiki/Pat_Boone "Pat Boone") recorded sanitized versions of songs recorded by the likes of Fats Domino, Little Richard, the Flamingos and Ivory Joe Hunter. Later, as those songs became popular, the original artists' recordings received radio play as well.[[100]](#cite_note-101)

The cover versions were not necessarily straightforward imitations. For example, Bill Haley's incompletely [bowdlerized](/wiki/Expurgation "Expurgation") cover of "[Shake, Rattle and Roll](/wiki/Shake,_Rattle_and_Roll "Shake, Rattle and Roll")" transformed Big Joe Turner's humorous and racy tale of adult love into an energetic teen dance number,[[92]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGilliland1969show_4,_track_5-93)[[101]](#cite_note-102) while Georgia Gibbs replaced [Etta James](/wiki/Etta_James "Etta James")' tough, sarcastic vocal in "Roll With Me, Henry" (covered as "Dance With Me, Henry") with a perkier vocal more appropriate for an audience unfamiliar with the song to which James's song was an [answer](/wiki/Answer_song "Answer song"), [Hank Ballard](/wiki/Hank_Ballard "Hank Ballard")'s "Work With Me, Annie".[[102]](#cite_note-103) Presley's rock and roll version of "Hound Dog", taken mainly from a version recorded by the pop band [Freddie Bell and the Bellboys](/wiki/Freddie_Bell_and_the_Bellboys "Freddie Bell and the Bellboys"), was very different from the blues shouter that [Big Mama Thornton](/wiki/Big_Mama_Thornton "Big Mama Thornton") had recorded four years earlier.[[103]](#cite_note-104)[[104]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGilliland1969show_7,_track_4-105) Other white artists who recorded cover versions of rhythm and blues songs included Gale Storm (Smiley Lewis' "I Hear You Knockin'"), the Diamonds (The Gladiolas' "Little Darlin'" and Frankie Lymon & the Teenagers' "Why Do Fools Fall in Love?"), the Crew Cuts (the Chords' "Sh-Boom" and Nappy Brown's "Don't Be Angry"), the Fountain Sisters (The Jewels' "Hearts of Stone") and the Maguire Sisters (The Moonglows' "Sincerely").

## Decline and later developments

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Rock_and_roll&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Decline and later developments")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/12/Buddy_Holly_%26_The_Crickets_publicity_portrait_-_cropped.jpg/250px-Buddy_Holly_%26_The_Crickets_publicity_portrait_-_cropped.jpg)](/wiki/File:Buddy_Holly_%26_The_Crickets_publicity_portrait_-_cropped.jpg)

[Buddy Holly](/wiki/Buddy_Holly "Buddy Holly") and his band, [the Crickets](/wiki/The_Crickets "The Crickets")

Some commentators have suggested a decline of rock and roll starting in 1958.[[105]](#cite_note-106)[[106]](#cite_note-107) The retirement of [Little Richard](/wiki/Little_Richard "Little Richard") to become a preacher (October 1957), the departure of Presley for service in the [United States Army](/wiki/United_States_Army "United States Army") (March 1958), the scandal surrounding [Jerry Lee Lewis](/wiki/Jerry_Lee_Lewis "Jerry Lee Lewis")' marriage to his [thirteen-year-old cousin](/wiki/Myra_Gale_Brown "Myra Gale Brown") (May 1958), riots caused by [Bill Haley](/wiki/Bill_Haley "Bill Haley")'s ill-fated tour of Europe (October 1958), the deaths of [Buddy Holly](/wiki/Buddy_Holly "Buddy Holly"), [the Big Bopper](/wiki/The_Big_Bopper "The Big Bopper") and [Ritchie Valens](/wiki/Ritchie_Valens "Ritchie Valens") in [a plane crash](/wiki/The_Day_the_Music_Died "The Day the Music Died") (February 1959), the breaking of the [Payola](/wiki/Payola "Payola") scandal implicating major figures, including [Alan Freed](/wiki/Alan_Freed "Alan Freed"), in bribery and corruption in promoting individual acts or songs (November 1959), the arrest of [Chuck Berry](/wiki/Chuck_Berry "Chuck Berry") (December 1959), and the death of [Eddie Cochran](/wiki/Eddie_Cochran "Eddie Cochran") in a car crash (April 1960) gave a sense that the initial phase of rock and roll had come to an end.[[107]](#cite_note-Campbell2008-108)

During the late 1950s and early 1960s, the rawer sounds of Presley, [Gene Vincent](/wiki/Gene_Vincent "Gene Vincent"), [Jerry Lee Lewis](/wiki/Jerry_Lee_Lewis "Jerry Lee Lewis") and [Buddy Holly](/wiki/Buddy_Holly "Buddy Holly") were commercially superseded by a more polished, commercial style of rock and roll influenced pop music. Marketing frequently emphasized the physical looks of the artist rather than the music, contributing to the successful careers of [Ricky Nelson](/wiki/Ricky_Nelson "Ricky Nelson"), [Tommy Sands](/wiki/Tommy_Sands_(American_singer) "Tommy Sands (American singer)"), [Bobby Vee](/wiki/Bobby_Vee "Bobby Vee"), [Jimmy Clanton](/wiki/Jimmy_Clanton "Jimmy Clanton"), and the Philadelphia trio of [Bobby Rydell](/wiki/Bobby_Rydell "Bobby Rydell"), [Frankie Avalon](/wiki/Frankie_Avalon "Frankie Avalon"), and [Fabian](/wiki/Fabian_Forte "Fabian Forte"), who all became "teen idols".[[108]](#cite_note-109)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/77/Johnny_Rivers_1975.JPG/250px-Johnny_Rivers_1975.JPG)](/wiki/File:Johnny_Rivers_1975.JPG)

[Johnny Rivers](/wiki/Johnny_Rivers "Johnny Rivers") was a key 1960s rock artist known for hits like "Memphis" and his "Go-go" style.

Some music historians have also pointed to important and innovative developments that built on rock and roll in this period, including [multitrack recording](/wiki/Multitrack_recording "Multitrack recording"), developed by [Les Paul](/wiki/Les_Paul "Les Paul"), the electronic treatment of sound by such innovators as [Joe Meek](/wiki/Joe_Meek "Joe Meek"), and the "[Wall of Sound](/wiki/Wall_of_Sound "Wall of Sound")" productions of [Phil Spector](/wiki/Phil_Spector "Phil Spector"),[[109]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGilliland1969show_21-110) continued desegregation of the charts, the rise of [surf music](/wiki/Surf_music "Surf music"), [garage rock](/wiki/Garage_rock "Garage rock") and the [Twist](/wiki/Twist_(dance) "Twist (dance)") dance craze.[[46]](#cite_note-KeightleyR&R-47) [Surf rock](/wiki/Surf_music "Surf music") in particular, noted for the use of reverb-drenched guitars, became one of the most popular forms of American rock of the early 1960s.[[110]](#cite_note-111)

While the sounds of the [British Invasion](/wiki/British_Invasion "British Invasion") would become the superseding forms of rock music during the mid-1960s, a few American artists were nonetheless able to achieve chart successes with rock and roll recordings during this time. The most notable of these was [Johnny Rivers](/wiki/Johnny_Rivers "Johnny Rivers"), who with hits such as "Memphis" enjoyed significant success in spite of the ongoing British Invasion.[[111]](#cite_note-112)[[112]](#cite_note-113) Another example was [Bobby Fuller](/wiki/Bobby_Fuller "Bobby Fuller") and his group [The Bobby Fuller Four](/wiki/The_Bobby_Fuller_Four "The Bobby Fuller Four"), who were especially inspired by Buddy Holly and stuck with a rock and roll style, scoring their most notable hit with ["I Fought the Law"](/wiki/I_Fought_the_Law#Bobby_Fuller_Four_version "I Fought the Law") (1965).[[113]](#cite_note-114)[[114]](#cite_note-115)[[115]](#cite_note-116)

## British rock and roll

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Rock_and_roll&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: British rock and roll")]

Main article: [British rock and roll](/wiki/British_rock_and_roll "British rock and roll")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/c7/Tommy_Steele_1957.jpg/250px-Tommy_Steele_1957.jpg)](/wiki/File:Tommy_Steele_1957.jpg)

[Tommy Steele](/wiki/Tommy_Steele "Tommy Steele"), one of the first British rock and rollers, performing in Stockholm in 1957

In the 1950s, Britain was well placed to receive American rock and roll music and culture.[[116]](#cite_note-Unterberger-117) It shared [a common language](/wiki/English_language "English language"), had been exposed to American culture through the stationing of troops in the country, and shared many social developments, including the emergence of distinct youth sub-cultures, which in Britain included the [Teddy Boys](/wiki/Teddy_Boy "Teddy Boy") and the [rockers](/wiki/Rocker_(subculture) "Rocker (subculture)").[[117]](#cite_note-D._O'Sullivan,_1974_pp._38–9-118) [Trad jazz](/wiki/Trad_jazz "Trad jazz") became popular in the UK, and many of its musicians were influenced by related American styles, including [boogie woogie](/wiki/Boogie_woogie "Boogie woogie") and the [blues](/wiki/Blues "Blues").[[118]](#cite_note-119) The [skiffle](/wiki/Skiffle "Skiffle") craze, led by [Lonnie Donegan](/wiki/Lonnie_Donegan "Lonnie Donegan"), used amateurish versions of American folk songs and encouraged many of the subsequent generation of rock and roll, folk, R&B and beat musicians to start performing.[[119]](#cite_note-Broken2003-120) At the same time British audiences were beginning to encounter American rock and roll, initially through films including *[Blackboard Jungle](/wiki/Blackboard_Jungle "Blackboard Jungle")* (1955) and *[Rock Around the Clock](/wiki/Rock_Around_the_Clock_(film) "Rock Around the Clock (film)")* (1956).[[120]](#cite_note-121) Both movies featured the [Bill Haley & His Comets](/wiki/Bill_Haley_%26_His_Comets "Bill Haley & His Comets") hit "[Rock Around the Clock](/wiki/Rock_Around_the_Clock "Rock Around the Clock")", which first entered the British charts in early 1955 – four months before it reached the [US pop charts](/wiki/Billboard_Hot_100 "Billboard Hot 100") – topped the British charts later that year and again in 1956 and helped identify rock and roll with teenage delinquency.[[121]](#cite_note-122)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/32/Cliff_Richard_1960.jpg/250px-Cliff_Richard_1960.jpg)](/wiki/File:Cliff_Richard_1960.jpg)

[Cliff Richard](/wiki/Cliff_Richard "Cliff Richard") became an early British rock and roll star with his 1958 hit "Move It".

The initial response of the British music industry was to attempt to produce copies of American records, recorded with session musicians and often fronted by teen idols. More grass roots British rock and rollers soon began to appear, including [Wee Willie Harris](/wiki/Wee_Willie_Harris "Wee Willie Harris") and [Tommy Steele](/wiki/Tommy_Steele "Tommy Steele").[[116]](#cite_note-Unterberger-117) During this period American Rock and Roll remained dominant but in 1958 Britain produced its first "authentic" rock and roll song and star, when [Cliff Richard](/wiki/Cliff_Richard "Cliff Richard") reached number 2 in the charts with "[Move It](/wiki/Move_It "Move It")".[[122]](#cite_note-123) At the same time, TV shows such as *[Six-Five Special](/wiki/Six-Five_Special "Six-Five Special")* and *[Oh Boy!](/wiki/Oh_Boy!_(TV_series) "Oh Boy! (TV series)")* promoted the careers of British rock and rollers like [Marty Wilde](/wiki/Marty_Wilde "Marty Wilde") and [Adam Faith](/wiki/Adam_Faith "Adam Faith").[[116]](#cite_note-Unterberger-117) Cliff Richard and his backing band, [the Shadows](/wiki/The_Shadows "The Shadows"), were the most successful home grown rock and roll based acts of the era.[[123]](#cite_note-124) Other leading acts included [Billy Fury](/wiki/Billy_Fury "Billy Fury"), [Joe Brown](/wiki/Joe_Brown_(singer) "Joe Brown (singer)"), and [Johnny Kidd & the Pirates](/wiki/Johnny_Kidd_%26_the_Pirates "Johnny Kidd & the Pirates"), whose 1960 hit song "[Shakin' All Over](/wiki/Shakin%27_All_Over "Shakin' All Over")" became a rock and roll standard.[[116]](#cite_note-Unterberger-117)

As interest in rock and roll was beginning to subside in America in the late 1950s and early 1960s, it was taken up by groups in British cities like [Liverpool](/wiki/Liverpool "Liverpool"), [Manchester](/wiki/Manchester "Manchester"), [Birmingham](/wiki/Birmingham "Birmingham"), and [London](/wiki/London "London").[[124]](#cite_note-Harry-125) About the same time, a [British blues](/wiki/British_blues "British blues") scene developed, initially led by purist blues followers such as [Alexis Korner](/wiki/Alexis_Korner "Alexis Korner") and [Cyril Davies](/wiki/Cyril_Davies "Cyril Davies") who were inspired by American musicians such as [Robert Johnson](/wiki/Robert_Johnson "Robert Johnson"), [Muddy Waters](/wiki/Muddy_Waters "Muddy Waters") and [Howlin' Wolf](/wiki/Howlin%27_Wolf "Howlin' Wolf").[[125]](#cite_note-Allmusic700-126) Many groups moved towards the [beat music](/wiki/Beat_music "Beat music") of rock and roll and rhythm and blues from skiffle, like the [Quarrymen](/wiki/Quarrymen "Quarrymen") who became [the Beatles](/wiki/The_Beatles "The Beatles"), producing a form of rock and roll revivalism that carried them and many other groups to national success from about 1963 and to international success from 1964, known in America as the British Invasion.[[126]](#cite_note-AllMusicBI-127) Groups that followed the Beatles included the beat-influenced [Freddie and the Dreamers](/wiki/Freddie_and_the_Dreamers "Freddie and the Dreamers"), [Wayne Fontana](/wiki/Wayne_Fontana "Wayne Fontana") and [the Mindbenders](/wiki/The_Mindbenders "The Mindbenders"), [Herman's Hermits](/wiki/Herman%27s_Hermits "Herman's Hermits") and [the Dave Clark Five](/wiki/The_Dave_Clark_Five "The Dave Clark Five").[[127]](#cite_note-Britannica-128) Early [British rhythm and blues](/wiki/British_rhythm_and_blues "British rhythm and blues") groups with more blues influences include [the Animals](/wiki/The_Animals "The Animals"), [the Rolling Stones](/wiki/The_Rolling_Stones "The Rolling Stones"), and [the Yardbirds](/wiki/The_Yardbirds "The Yardbirds").[[128]](#cite_note-129)

## Cultural influence

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Rock_and_roll&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Cultural influence")]

Main article: [Social effects of rock music](/wiki/Social_effects_of_rock_music "Social effects of rock music")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/89/Emerson_Model_888_Pioneer_8-Transistor_AM_Radio%2C_Made_in_the_USA%2C_Circa_1958_%2821973868670%29.jpg/250px-Emerson_Model_888_Pioneer_8-Transistor_AM_Radio%2C_Made_in_the_USA%2C_Circa_1958_%2821973868670%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Emerson_Model_888_Pioneer_8-Transistor_AM_Radio,_Made_in_the_USA,_Circa_1958_(21973868670).jpg)

The [transistor radio](/wiki/Transistor_radio "Transistor radio") (1958 Emerson pictured) introduced [FM radio](/wiki/FM_broadcasting "FM broadcasting") and rock and roll music to [baby boomer](/wiki/Baby_boomers "Baby boomers") children in American households.

G. C. Altschuler notes that rock and roll influenced lifestyles, fashion, attitudes, and language,[[129]](#cite_note-130) possibly even contributing to the civil rights movement since both African-American and European-American teens enjoyed the music.[[13]](#cite_note-Altshuler2003p35-14)

William J. Schafer states that the lyrics of rock and roll songs, dealing with issues such as cars, school, dating, and clothing, described events and conflicts to which most listeners could relate through personal experience. Topics such as sex that had generally been considered taboo began to appear in rock and roll lyrics. This led to the music breaking boundaries and expressing emotions that people were actually feeling but had not discussed openly, leading to an awakening in American youth culture.[[130]](#cite_note-Schafer,_William_J_1972-131)

### Race

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Rock_and_roll&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Race")]

In the crossover of African-American "race music" to a growing white youth audience, the popularization of rock and roll involved both black performers reaching a white audience and white musicians performing African-American music.[[131]](#cite_note-132) Rock and roll appeared at a time when racial tensions in the United States were entering a new phase, with the beginnings of the [civil rights](/wiki/Civil_rights "Civil rights") movement for [desegregation](/wiki/Racial_segregation "Racial segregation"), leading to the [U.S. Supreme Court](/wiki/U.S._Supreme_Court "U.S. Supreme Court") ruling that abolished the policy of "[separate but equal](/wiki/Separate_but_equal "Separate but equal")" in 1954, but leaving a policy which would be extremely difficult to enforce in parts of the United States.[[132]](#cite_note-133) The coming together of white youth audiences and [black music](/wiki/African_American_music "African American music") in rock and roll inevitably provoked strong white racist reactions within the US, with many whites condemning its breaking down of barriers based on color.[[13]](#cite_note-Altshuler2003p35-14) According to M. T. Bertrand, many observers saw rock and roll as heralding the way for desegregation in creating a new form of music that encouraged racial cooperation and shared experience.[[133]](#cite_note-134) Mina Carson argued that early rock and roll was instrumental in the way both white and black teenagers identified themselves.[[134]](#cite_note-135)

### Teen culture

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Rock_and_roll&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: Teen culture")]

Main article: [Youth subculture](/wiki/Youth_subculture "Youth subculture")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/ac/True_Life_Romance_3.jpg/250px-True_Life_Romance_3.jpg)](/wiki/File:True_Life_Romance_3.jpg)

"There's No Romance in Rock and Roll" made the cover of *True Life Romance* in 1956.

Ruth Padel claimed that rock and roll was one of the first music genres to define an [age group](/wiki/Age_group "Age group").[[135]](#cite_note-padel-136) It gave teenagers a sense of belonging, even when they were alone.[[135]](#cite_note-padel-136) Rock and roll emerged with teen culture among the first [baby boomer](/wiki/Baby_boomer "Baby boomer") generation, who had greater relative affluence and leisure time and adopted rock and roll as part of a distinct subculture.[[136]](#cite_note-Coleman2007-137) This involved not just music, absorbed via radio, record buying, jukeboxes and TV programs like *[American Bandstand](/wiki/American_Bandstand "American Bandstand")*, but also extended to film, clothes, hair, cars and motorcycles, and distinctive language. The youth culture exemplified by rock and roll was a recurring source of concern for older generations, who worried about juvenile delinquency and social rebellion, particularly because, to a large extent, rock and roll culture was shared by different racial and social groups.[[136]](#cite_note-Coleman2007-137)

In America, that concern was conveyed even in youth cultural artifacts such as [comic books](/wiki/Comic_books "Comic books"). In "There's No Romance in Rock and Roll" from *True Life Romance* (1956), a defiant teen dates a rock and roll-loving boy but drops him for one who likes traditional adult music—to her parents' relief.[[137]](#cite_note-138) In Britain, where postwar prosperity was more limited, rock and roll culture became attached to the pre-existing [Teddy Boy](/wiki/Teddy_Boy "Teddy Boy") movement, largely working class in origin, and eventually to the [rockers](/wiki/Rocker_(subculture) "Rocker (subculture)").[[117]](#cite_note-D._O'Sullivan,_1974_pp._38–9-118) "On the white side of the deeply segregated music market", rock and roll became marketed for teenagers, as in [Dion and the Belmonts](/wiki/Dion_and_the_Belmonts "Dion and the Belmonts")' "[A Teenager in Love](/wiki/A_Teenager_in_Love "A Teenager in Love")" (1959).[[138]](#cite_note-139)

### Dance styles

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Rock_and_roll&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: Dance styles")]

From its early 1950s beginnings through the early 1960s, rock and roll spawned new [dance crazes](/wiki/Novelty_and_fad_dances "Novelty and fad dances")[[139]](#cite_note-140) including the [twist](/wiki/Twist_(dance) "Twist (dance)"). Teenagers found the syncopated [backbeat](/wiki/Backbeat "Backbeat") rhythm especially suited to reviving Big Band-era [jitterbug](/wiki/Jitterbug "Jitterbug") dancing. [Sock hops](/wiki/Sock_hop "Sock hop"), school and church gym dances, and home basement dance parties became the rage, and American teens watched [Dick Clark](/wiki/Dick_Clark "Dick Clark")'s *[American Bandstand](/wiki/American_Bandstand "American Bandstand")* to keep up on the latest dance and fashion styles.[[140]](#cite_note-141) From the mid-1960s on, as "rock and roll" was rebranded as "rock", later dance genres followed, leading to [funk](/wiki/Funk "Funk"), [disco](/wiki/Disco "Disco"), [house](/wiki/House_music "House music"), [techno](/wiki/Techno "Techno"), and [hip hop](/wiki/Hip_hop "Hip hop").[[141]](#cite_note-142)
